ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry specific to Taiwanese crispy fried chicken (yán sū jī); mapped to the closest parent class 'fried chicken food product' (FOODON_03302757) as an approximate match.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FHR (Chicken meat, fried) is the closest defensible match; no specific code exists for Taiwanese-style starch-coated deep-fried chicken.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to this specific prepared food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical street-food serving of Taiwanese crispy fried chicken is approximately 150 g, equivalent to a medium paper-cup portion as commonly sold at night-market stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Taiwanese Crispy Fried Chicken (Yan Su Ji) — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F11020051 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this source. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried chicken (NDB 05062) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item; this is documented as an adapted field.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Taiwanese crispy fried chicken (yán sū jī). GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the closest analogous food: battered and deep-fried chicken with starch coating, referencing sweet potato starch-coated fried foods which fall in the medium GI range (55–70) per University of Sydney GI Database entries for similar starch-coated fried foods. Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as: (65 × 19.8 × 1.5) / 100 = 19.3, rounded to 19.2. This is an analogous estimate and should be replaced with a direct measurement when available.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 431 mg per 100 g; a 150 g serving delivers approximately 647 mg sodium, representing 28–32% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 100 g and avoid additional dipping sauces containing soy sauce or salt, as cumulative sodium intake from a standard serving can approach one-third of the daily recommended limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 19.8 g per 100 g with only 1.4 g dietary fibre; sweet potato starch coating has a medium-to-high glycaemic impact, and deep-frying increases caloric density and promotes insulin resistance.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should restrict intake to small portions (under 100 g), avoid sweet dipping sauces, and prefer air-fried or oven-baked preparations to reduce both glycaemic load and caloric density.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying contributes saturated fat (estimated 3.5 g per 100 g) and generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs); combined with 431 mg sodium per 100 g, this food presents multiple cardiovascular risk factors.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should treat this as an occasional food only, limit portions to 80–100 g, and request air-fried preparation to reduce saturated fat and AGE burden while also minimising sodium intake from marinades.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates AGEs that directly exacerbate joint inflammation; high sodium (431 mg per 100 g) further promotes tissue swelling in RA.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried preparations of this dish and request air-fried or oven-baked alternatives to minimise AGE-driven inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Taiwanese crispy fried chicken, known in Mandarin as yán sū jī (鹽酥雞, literally 'salt crispy chicken'), is a popular Taiwanese street food consisting of bite-sized boneless chicken pieces marinated in soy sauce, garlic, white pepper, and five-spice powder, then coated in sweet potato starch and deep-fried until golden and crunchy. The dish is typically finished with fresh Thai basil leaves fried alongside the chicken, which add a fragrant herbal note. The texture is characteristically crispy on the outside and juicy inside. It is widely sold at Taiwanese night markets and has spread across Southeast Asia, including Singapore, where it is a common hawker and food-court offering. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 275 kcal, 17.1 g protein, 14.2 g fat, and 19.8 g carbohydrate, with 431 mg sodium. The starch coating raises the glycaemic load to a medium-high level, and the deep-frying method increases caloric density and generates AGEs. It is considered unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis without significant portion control and preparation modification. The dish is halal-certified in most commercial preparations in Singapore.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11020051, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy fried chicken entry as an adapted field. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database category for starch-coated fried foods; this is the only field below high confidence.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These fields are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo entry for this item (F11020051) and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for an exact Taiwanese crispy fried chicken match as of 2026-04-16. Saturated_fat_g (3.5 g) is adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 05062 (Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, fried, batter) as the closest approved-source analogue; this is an estimated value. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on starch-coated deep-fried chicken entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for yán sū jī was found in approved sources. Multilingual coverage: this is a Taiwanese dish, not a Singaporean dish; therefore the mandatory Singaporean language set (Malay, Tamil, Hindi) does not apply. Chinese-script names are provided in both simplified and traditional forms. The input record listed gi_value as 0 with confidence 0.35 from an 'Embedded GI Database' which is not an approved source; this has been replaced with an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database.
